title: practical english usage - Part 216
content:
‘ I can whistle through my fingers.' ‘ Can you, dear?

‘ Have you finished? ‘ Yes, I have.' question tags (►  305-306).

Ellipsis is used regularly in short answers (►  308), reply questions (►  307) and 2 short    answers,      etc:  Yes,    I have.

‘ Who has a dictionary? ‘ I have.' ‘ I'm tired.' ‘ I am too.' ‘ Who's the driver? ‘ I am.' This also happens after non-auxiliary be and have.

‘ You wouldn't have won if I hadn't helped you.' ‘ Yes, I would (have).' ‘ You're not trying very hard.' ‘ I am.' ' I've forgotten the address.' ‘ I have too.' I can't see you today, but I can tomorrow. (= ... 1 can see you ...) after the auxiliary.

Other words, as well as the rest of the verb phrase, can be left out He said he would arrive before seven, and he did.

I may come to London. I'll phone you if I do.

Do can be used before ellipsis if there is no other auxiliary to repeat.

‘ Shall I tell him  what I think? ‘ I wouldn't if I were you.' I'll come and see you when I can. (= ... can come and see you.) He said he'd write, but he hasn't. (= ... hasn’ t written.) ‘ Get up.' 7 am /æm//     (= I am getting up.) except in negatives.

pronunciation (►  315), and contractions (►  337)  are not normally used phrase, if the meaning is clear. The  auxiliary verb usually has a 'strong’ We   can avoid repetition by using an auxiliary verb instead of a complete verb 1  auxiliary       instead      of  complete       verb    phrase 279 ellipsis     after    auxiliary      verbs    __ not The most important thing is to keep calm, (     The-most important is to...) not Poor little boy! (     Poor little!) Note that nouns are not normally dropped in other situations.

4 other     situations I'm going to call in at the doctor's on the way home.

Could you pick up some chops from the butcher's?

We spent the weekend at Jack and Emily's.

are often used without following nouns.

When  we  talk about people’ s homes, and about shops and offices, possessives We're going to see Hamlet' at the Mermaid. (= ... the Mermaid Theatre.) He's staying at the Hilton. (= ... the Hilton Hotel.) (=... the Beethoven violin concerto with the Scottish National Orchestra...) She's playing the Beethoven with the Scottish National tomorrow night.

The  last words of well-known names are often dropped.

3 well-known        names Information StructureSection 25 grammar •  280  ellipsis: infinitives ’ Somebody ought to clean up the bathroom.’ ’ I'll ask lack to.’ Sorry I shouted at you. I didn't mean to.

I don't dance much  now, but I used to a lot.

’ Let's go for a walk.’ ’ I don’t want to.’ ’ Are you and Gillian getting married? ’ We hope to.' words), if the meaning is clear.

We   can use to instead of the whole infinitive of a repeated verb (and following 1  to   used    instead      of  whole     infinitive:      W e  hope      to.

280 ellipsis:    i nfinitives       _   ____ For do so, ►    29.

’ Do you think he'll phone? ’ He might do.' (AmE  ... He might.) by do. For details, ►    28.

In British English, a main verb that is left out after an auxiliary can be replaced 6 substitution         with    do:   He  might       do.

’ Emily should be told.’ ’ She must  be.' (More natural than ... She must.) modal auxiliary.

And we  normally include a second auxiliary verb after a change of 'I think Emily should be told.' ’ She has been.' (More natural than ... She has.) same form.

We   often include a second auxiliary verb if it has not appeared before in the have been.’ ’ Could you have been dreaming? ’ I suppose I could / COULD have / COULD However, more auxiliaries can be included. The  first is stressed.

’ You wouldn't have enjoyed the film.' ’ Yes, I would.' (= ... I would have When there is more than one auxiliary, ellipsis usually happens after the first.

5  more    than    one   auxiliary:      / could      have      been.

If you prefer, we can go tomorrow instead.

If you could, I'd like you to help me this evening.

If you can, call me when you arrive.

beginning with if.

it can sometimes happen the other way round. This is common in sentences the complete form has already been used once (see above examples). However, Ellipsis normally happens when an expression is used for a second time, after 4 ellipsis     before     complete       form:   If  you     can,    call    me    ...
